segment-text: "MAN ON A LEDGE"

segment-speaker: Pablo Fenjves Revisions by Chris Gorak
segment-text: Current Revisions by Erich Hoeber & Jon Hoeber
10/7/10 - White Draft
EXT. 49TH STREET & 6TH AVENUE - EARLY MORNING

A bus stops, doors opening with a pneumatic HISS. exits and starts down the street. A man NICK CASSIDY is 35, with a determined gaze, strong and lean, clean shaven, wearing a rumpled suit. He could be a stockbroker or in sales; he has that tired but intense look.
EXT. ROOSEVELT HOTEL - ESTABLISHING
Nick checks his watch, 6:23, then heads for the canopied entrance of this two tower, twenty-five story hotel in the heart of the city.
INT. ROOSEVELT HOTEL - LOBBY - CONTINUOUS

A SLEEPY BELLHOP, 65, feeble, probably allowed to keep his job out of pity, sees Nick coming and opens the door wide. Nick nods, unsmiling as he enters. Several GUESTS sit in the plush marble lobby, reading the morning paper, sipping coffee. Nick heads for the front desk. The HOTEL MANAGER, 50 prepares for the morning crush.
Good morning, sir. Morning.
Last name's Walker.
Yes, Mr. Walker. Room 2105, East Tower. Great view.
He types a couple of codes into his computer.
We also have a larger unit available on the east side I can offer to you at the same price.
Nick shakes his head.
I'll stick with the view.
Of course. He signals for the bellhop.
INT. ROOM 2105 - DAY
The bellhop enters, followed by Nick. It's a good-sized room with a set of large windows. bellhop opens the curtains and adjusts the lights. The
What's your best Champagne? Bollinger '97, Vieille Vignes. finest. Anything else, Sir?
INT. ROOM 2105 - DAY
Nick relaxes, champagne in a bucket, uncorked, a half eaten filet mignon, a plate of caviar. It's an over-the-top fourstar breakfast. He pulls at a lobster tail, dips it in melted butter.
INT. ROOM 2105 - BATHROOM - DAY
Splashing water on his face, Nick takes a moment, staring himself down in the mirror. Thinking? Psyching himself up? Hard to tell, but it's intense.
INT. ROOM 2105 - DAY

Nick walks in from the bathroom with a hand towel and goes to work methodically wiping down everything he's touched: the card-key, table, chair, utensils. Moving to the desk, he methodically writes three lines. Leaving the note, he walks to the windows. spectacular. The view is
EXT. ROOM 2105 - CONTINUOUS
High above the street. Nick stands framed head to toe in a window, looking out at the city for a long beat. He opens the window... Then steps out, onto the narrow ledge running around the
building.

The wind tousles his hair. Leaning slightly forward, Nick looks down at the stomachtightening, ball-cinching view of the street 200 feet below. Twenty-one floors above certain death, he's poised to jump. Far below, a WOMAN SCREAMS.
EXT. PRISON YARD - DAY

WHACK! Nick, a prison inmate, bearded and unkempt, throws a hard elbow square into ANOTHER PRISONER'S face... ...sending a METAL SHANK chattering across concrete. A SECOND PRISONER grabs Nick from behind in a choke-hold as a THIRD PRISONER delivers gut-wrenching blows. The fight is sloppy and raw and real as Nick pushes back, sending himself and the guy on him falling into a rack of weights. CRACK! The first guy clobbers Nick with a weight bar SNAPPING his arm and knocking him down. He closes in for the kill, but Nick kicks him in the balls.
SUPERED TITLE: SING SING CORRECTIONAL FACILITY, NEW YORK

The third guy kicks Nick in the side, flipping him over, and the others surround him, kicking and stomping. ALARMS SOUND and prisoners move to the edge of the yard. Guards race in... but the only thing to see is Nick lying unconscious in a pool of blood.
INT. NICK'S CELL - NIGHT
Nick sits alone on his bed. He's got a cast on one arm, ugly bruises and swelling everywhere. He stares out at nothing: a million miles from freedom.
INT. PRISON - INTERVIEW ROOM - DAY
A female PRISON PSYCHIATRIST, 50, cold and detached, sits across from Nick, thumbing his file. Nick's still in the cast, but his wounds have healed a bit.
Nick stares off blankly, trying to quell his emotion.
Your old life is gone. (beat) Not many men, let alone ex-cops, can handle a forty-year sentence.
Despair creeps across Nick's face.
Maybe I'm one of the ones who can't handle it.
This is good. Recognizing you've lost hope is the first step toward accepting the reality of your situation.
He just stares off.
Nick, have you ever considered hurting yourself?
Hurting? No. Killing? goddamned day.
She coldly scratches a note in his file.
INT. NICK'S CELL - DAY
An INMATE arrives outside Nick's cell with a cart of books. Nick returns one: "The Psychology of Depression."
EXT. PRISON YARD - DAY
Nick steps out into the harsh light of the prison yard. The group that nearly killed him before stands across the way, eyeing Nick as a guard shoves him forward.
INT. PRISON - VISITATION ROOM - DAY
PRISON GUARDS keep watch. across long steel tables.
PRISONERS speak to LOVED ONES

Nick waits at a table, hands cuffed in front of him, playing with a RUBIK'S CUBE. His cast is off now, but he's got a fresh black eye and bruises. He looks up. MIKE CAVANAUGH, 30s, sharply dressed in a suit and tie approaches to greet him. Hey, Cass.
Nick takes in Mike's attire as he sits down.
Missed you, Mikey.
Been awhile.
Yeah, well. Getting married...
Nick smiles bitterly.
I expect everyone else to dry up and blow away. Not you.
Mike looks away.
Hey. Look at me. It's not right I hear from someone else you made detective. Mike gets it.
It's hard for me, Cass, seeing you like this.
I know, Mikey. (beat) Congratulations.
It's a nice moment, but then Mike turns serious.
Your brother called me.
Nick sets down the cube.
He ask for money? lost the house.
I heard they
Your dad is dying. There it is, hard and ugly. How long?
Mike shrugs. Nick looks away, choked up.
Not long.
I'll talk to the warden, make sure you get a day on the outside with no hassle.
Nick smiles grimly. mindlessly. He picks up the Rubik's Cube, turning it
EXT. PRISON YARD - DAY

Nick jogs along the razor wire fence, head down, keeping to himself, burning through a rigorous routine. Suddenly a PRISON GUARD stands in his path. Nick stops, staring at the guy. He knows what's coming.
I/E. SING SING PRISON - GATE - DAY

A prisoner transport vehicle stands at inspection. A PRISON GUARD drives, racked shotgun beside him. In the cage in the back are another GUARD and Nick, who sits handcuffed to a steel rail, wearing his prison blues.
Beautiful day.
The transport pulls away from the prison.
EXT. CEDAR LAWN CEMETERY - SUNSET

Set up against a set of train tracks, there's no rolling hills or shady trees here: just brown grass and fumes. Nick stands, hands cuffed before him, next to his brother, JOEY CASSIDY, 30, clean-shaven, a touch brainy with a youthful charm. Neither wants to be here. next to the other. And neither wants to be standing JOEY'S GIRLFRIEND, ANGIE, 24, holds Joey close, quiet and supportive. Later we'll get to know her as an ass-kicking, take-no-shit New York firecracker: down to earth and charming but emotional, the kind of woman you don't want to cross. Mike's there with his WIFE, looking slick compared to the rumpled older generation of MOURNERS who watch as FATHER LEO, 60, wraps it up.
...and when a good soul is taken from us, it can be hard to find reason to believe. But, having
faith in God's promise Father Leo looks to Nick; his words are for him...
- and faith in yourself - is your one true ally.
Father Leo turns to bless FRANK CASSIDY'S CASKET hovering over the hole.
May God bless you, Frank Cassidy; you are truly free. Amen.
A brief round of Amens.
Less than twenty feet away, the prison transport vehicle
idles. Both prison guards stand at the ready. Nick and Joey say good-bye to his father's friends. Not allowed physical contact, Nick is scrutinized by the guards. The moment's surreal. Thanks...
Thank you....
Mike waits his turn, then approaches. Cass.
Sorry, man.
Nick nods.
Thanks for coming, Mikey.
JANICE, Mike's wife, conservatively dressed but gorgeous, gives him a smile.
It's nice to meet you, Nick. heard so much. I've
Mike hangs back with Nick as Janice moves on.
You finally got a woman with all
her teeth. Mike grins.
Anyway I can help, you guys let me know.
He leans in, respecting the rules but too close for the guards' liking.
I'll be there. Okay?
Nick nods. Mike pats Joey on the shoulder as he walks away. Angie steps in and gives Nick a peck on the cheek.
No contact!
I'm glad it's the pretty girl who broke the rules.
Angie smiles.
You got the time?
(checks her phone)

She kisses Joey and heads back to her car, leaving the brothers alone at the casket. Nick stares out, watching Mike and the other guests pulling out, heading back to their lives. A TRAIN WHISTLE sounds in the distance and Nick glances at the tracks with obvious displeasure.
This the best you could do? of the goddamned tracks?
The prison guards perk up at his tone.
(disgusted) You killed him the day you went to prison.
Nick smiles bitterly.
Cause I left him with a goddammed screw-up.
Joey burns with rage.
My life ended the day you went down. College: gone. All I did is earn for your defense and dad's medical.
Yeah well, apparently not enough.

Joey explodes, swinging hard, hitting him in the face. Hands cuffed together, Nick clubs Joey across the jaw. Joey retaliates with a hard left-hook, knocking Nick down, then jumps on him throwing more punches.
This is all your fault.

Nick manages to hit Joey again, slamming him to one side. The guards jump in, pulling them apart. his hands still cuffed. Nick's dragged away, Joey lunges for him again, but the second guard blocks him. Walk away!
PRISON GUARD #2
Joey looks after Nick, still pissed, spits, and walks off. Nick stops struggling, taking a final look at his father's casket. The guard shoves him toward the truck.
You're done. Let's go.

As Nick reaches the truck, he glances up the tracks as the train WHISTLE sounds again... ...then suddenly turns, swinging one arm over the guard, flicking open A SWITCHBLADE and jamming it against the guy's neck as he backs against the truck for protection. The second guard draws and aims, but he doesn't have a shot. Drop it!
PRISON GUARD #2
Nick's blade is already digging deep, drawing blood...
Stay back, Donnie. (to his hostage) Unlock 'em.
(into radio) This is three twelve, prisoner escape in progress at Cedar Lawn! He's got my partner, I need backup!
With shaking hands, guard #1 unlocks the cuffs... ...and Nick instantly pulls his Glock 22, putting it to his head.
NOW!
PRISON GUARD #2
Take it easy, Cassidy. He drops his weapon and raises his hands. Joey watches from a distance as Nick pushes the guards into the truck.
INT. PRISON TRANSPORT VEHICLE - CONTINUOUS
Nick quickly cuffs the guards to the rail.
You won't get far.
I don't plan to.
EXT. CEDAR LAWN CEMETERY - CONTINUOUS
Joey runs up as Nick hurries out of the truck.
The hell are you doing?
Nick levels the Glock at his brother's head. Keys.
The guards watch as he pushes Joey towards the parking lot.
EXT. PARKING LOT - SUNSET
Joey stands helpless as his brother unlocks his 4x4.
You sure about this? going back.
There's no He glances at the far end of the cemetery where two police cars enter, then back over at the APPROACHING TRAIN. Yeah.

Jumping in the truck, Nick peels out. Joey stands there staring after him as the two police cruisers blow past him. I/E. JOEY'S TRUCK - DRIVING - CONTINUOUS Nick floors it, flying through the cemetery, eyeing the train and the cops. Breaking through a chain-link fence, he maneuvers the truck up the berm and across the tracks... ...as the train roars past, WHISTLE BLARING... ...cutting off the cruisers.
EXT. EMPTY ROAD - SUNSET - ESTABLISHING

Nick's truck flies down the road. I/E. JOEY'S TRUCK - DRIVING - CONTINUOUS One hand on the wheel, he goes through the glove-box. Coming up with a flip-blade knife, he cuts off his GPS ANKLEBRACELET and tosses it out the window. Zipping open Joey's gym bag on the front seat, he rifles through it, gunning the truck faster. In the distance, far behind him, the lights of police pursuit rise. Nick eyes them drawing closer, then turns off on a side road, blowing past a sign that reads "NO OUTLET."
EXT. WASTEWATER TREATMENT PLANT - ESTABLISHING
This massive plant sprawls across hundred of acres: a maze of pipes and tanks.
EXT. WASTEWATER TREATMENT PLANT - SUNSET
The 4x4 sits abandoned, as police cars pour in surrounding it, SCREECHING to a stop. Cops swarm out, guns ready. of ways.
(into radio) Set a perimeter. gone far.
Nick could have gone any number He can't have BOOM DOWN to REVEAL that he's standing on a manhole.
EXT. WOODED HILLSIDE - ACCESS DOORWAY - NIGHT

This concrete-framed gated steel door set into this wooded hillside swings open and Nick emerges. Pulling on a coat from Joey's gym bag, he disappears into the night. BOOM UP to REVEAL a sea of blue and red flashing lights at the treatment plant a mile away.
I/E. MIKE'S SUV - MOVING - NIGHT
Mike drives, Manhattan's skyscrapers visible up ahead. freeway back into the city is thick with traffic. Janice touches up her make-up as Mike's phone RINGS.
(into phone) Cavanaugh.
INTERCUT - EXT. CEDAR LAWN CEMETERY - NIGHT - CONTINUOUS
Joey leans on a police car, the crime scene abuzz with activity behind him. It's Joey.
Nick's escaped.
Jacked the guards, took my truck.
Mike looks like he just got gut-punched.
I don't know what to do, Mike.
I got a guy with the Staties. Don't worry. I'll find him. Thanks.
Still stunned, Mike places the phone back on the dashboard.
Everything okay?
He smiles.
care of.
Nothing I can't take
EXT. STORAGE AREA - NIGHT - ESTABLISHING
Elevated train tracks run above this fenced off area where several cargo containers have been placed for storage.
EXT. STORAGE AREA - NIGHT

Hopping the fence, Nick spins the combination lock on a container and swings open the door. Grabbing an extension cord from inside, he climbs on top of the container, yanks the power cord from the roof of the next container, and plugs his in, lights flickering on.
INT. CARGO CONTAINER - NIGHT

As Nick shuts the door, we see the place is filled with furniture, luggage, and labelled moving boxes: his old life. Nick sits, exhausted. Utterly spent. Then he sees a framed picture lying on one of the boxes. He picks it up revealing his DAD, JOEY and HIMSELF on a fishing trip from years ago: the three of them smiling. He sits there, thinking about the past. About what to do. SERIES OF SHOTS: Nick's got a suitcase open, shaving off his goatee and scruff. He wipes his face. With a pair of scissors, he goes to work on his hair, cutting over the ears and off the collar. Digging to the bottom of a coffee can of loose pennies, Nick pulls out a money clip of crisp cash. He pulls a suit from a wardrobe box and starts changing: pants, shirt, a cheap Timex 1440 digital sports watch. Finally he slips the 4x6 photo from the frame and pockets it.
I/E. BUS - EARLY MORNING
Transformed, Nick rides into the city with the early-shift of working stiffs.
EXT. 49TH STREET & 6TH AVENUE - EARLY MORNING
The bus stops, doors opening with a pneumatic HISS. steps out.
This is where we opened.
EXT. 47TH STREET - EARLY MORNING

Nick walks along 47th, enjoying early morning serenity. The city is empty and calm: a rare moment before the insanity of eight million people begins again. This is the heart of the Diamond District, where virtually every sign and storefront here is a diamond or jewelry business. Nick pauses to check out his reflection in a store window: straightens his tie, tugs on his cuffs.
EXT. 45TH STREET & MADISON AVENUE - EARLY MORNING
Nick stops walking and looks up at the Roosevelt Hotel... ...and the ledge 21 floors above the street.
EXT. ROOM 2105 - DAY
Nick stands on the ledge as before, poised to jump, eyeing the street far below. A WOMAN SCREAMS.
EXT. 45TH STREET - DAY
A COP runs up the street towards the Roosevelt, looking up.
INT. MIDTOWN NORTH PRECINCT - DAY
A DESK SERGEANT relays commands over the radio.
EXT. 45TH STREET - DAY
OFFICERS unload BARRICADES, slamming them into place, one after another. The street is closed. Onlookers are corralled at both ends of the block, out of harm's way.
EXT. LEDGE - DAY
Nick looks down, watching the rapidly expanding action.
EXT. 45TH STREET - DAY

The swelling crowd parts as a FIRE TRUCK pulls in followed by a NYPD ESU CAR. DETECTIVE BOB DOUGHERTY gets out: 40, brash, self-confident, and New York Irish through and through. Dougherty and the firemen look at Nick 200 feet up.
That'll make a mess.
INT. ROOM 2105 - DAY
The bellhop unlocks the door, allowing Dougherty to enter.
EXT. LEDGE - CONTINUOUS
Dougherty pokes his head outside, glancing down 21 gutwrenching stories: Jesus. He looks over at Nick standing on the ledge, ten feet away, contemplating the abyss.
Mr. Walker? Hang tight, okay? My name is Bob Dougherty and I'm here to help.
Nick just stares down at the street.
EXT. 45TH STREET - DAY
DETECTIVE NATHAN MARCUS, 45, suit and tie, is the competent, intense, get-it-done man in charge: always in control and doesn't suffer fools. He heads into the Roosevelt trailed by two cops.
I want mobile command here pronto. Keep the street locked up. Businesses too. And get more crowd control. (into walkie) Dougherty, report.
INT. ROOSEVELT HOTEL - LOBBY - CONTINUOUS
Marcus points at the waiting manager as he sweeps into the hotel, crossing to the elevators.
Jumper's mid-thirties, looks professional. He's not talking.
(to the Manager)
I need the doorman, bellhop, room
Marcus enters the elevator, presses "21."
Yes sir.
The doors close on him.
INT. ROOM 2105 - DAY
Standing at the open window, Dougherty glances over as Marcus walks in. Out on the ledge, Nick checks his watch.
I want a woman.
Dougherty pauses. This is a new one.
Come inside, I'll get you whatever you like. Blonde? Brunette?
Officer Anderson. If she isn't here in 30 minutes, I'm jumping. You and I are done talking, Bob.
INT. ROOM 2105 - CONTINUOUS
Dougherty walks from the window, Marcus waiting.
Ready for this? He wants Anderson. Says he's going over if he doesn't
Marcus notes Nick's breakfast spread on the table.
Last Supper?
Not a good sign.
Marcus thinks it over. He points to the note on the desk.
"I will exit this world just like I entered - innocent. I'm going
home."
To the nut-house if he's lucky.
Get a print team in here. an ID. (beat) And call Anderson. Seriously?
You'll back her up.
Sure. Why not? Everyone loves a good train wreck.
Marcus walks out.
INT. LYDIA'S APARTMENT - DAY
Sprawled on the bed in her underwear, LYDIA ANDERSON wakes from the sound of her RINGING phone. She's 30s. Beautiful.
And a wreck.

Lydia stumbles through the simple apartment, tripping over a half-empty vodka bottle. The place is nicely decorated, but a mess. There are family pictures: parents, brothers, many of them cops. Pulling the phone from under a pizza box, she answers. Anderson.
Christ, did I wake you up?
Course not. Hold on.
Lowering the phone, she takes a beat to pull it together.
DOUGHERTY (V.O.)
This is the last thing she expected.
I'm not on the rotation. No shit. now.
The Roosevelt. Right
The line goes dead. She stands there, slightly lost, not sure what to do. Crap.
EXT. LEDGE - DAY
Nick checks his watch. THE CROWD BELOW continues to grow. Cops clear a path allowing a TACTICAL MOBILE COMMAND CENTER truck to roll in.
INT. MOBILE COMMAND CENTER - DAY
Two POLICE TECHS sit before a huge array of communications equipment and video monitors as Marcus walks in.
control, the precinct, and the
hotel.
Get cameras up. Where's my crime scene unit? POLICE TECH #2 Almost here.
Anything on J. Walker?
We've got over six hundred of 'em in the city. POLICE TECH #1 Fire Chief wants a word.
EXT. 45TH STREET - DAY
ELENA OTERO MORALES, 40, with smoldering good looks, eyes the gathering crowd then looks up to Nick on the ledge. Her CAMERAMAN frames them up.
Let's burn one.
Unlucky in love? Victim of the Stock Market? A young man
contemplates death. This is Elena Otero Morales reporting live from
EXT. LEDGE / 45TH STREET - DAY

Nick watches the ESU TRUCK pull up. The Emergency Services Unit jumps to it, hauling out an INFLATABLE PAD. One hits a switch; the emergency airbag begins to inflate. 20' x 20' x 8'. From this height it's not much bigger than a postage stamp.
EXT. 45TH STREET - CONTINUOUS
The ESU guys beside the inflatable pad look up at Nick, a tiny speck twenty floors up. Helluva a drop.
Cops push back the gathering crowd of construction workers, businesspeople, homeless, students: a cross-section of the entire city. A heavyset BEARDED GUY is annoyed.
Don't shove me, man.

Behind him, Lydia steps out of a cab, completely transformed and looking totally professional. She stares up 21 floors at Nick. It's a hard, internal moment, seeing him, knowing that she's about to become responsible for this man's unraveling life. Hey. She turns.
You good to work?
He's right there with the Fire Chief.
INT. ROOSEVELT HOTEL - LOBBY
Stopped by the officer controlling the front door, she presents her badge. Anderson.

At the announcement of her name it's like a hush ripples out, silence falling across the OFFICERS and EMERGENCY WORKERS filling the lobby. Head up, ignoring the dozens of pairs of eyes on her, Lydia crosses to the elevators, shoes CLIPPING loudly in the silence.
INT. ELEVATOR - CONTINUOUS
Hitting 21, she turns: every person in the room is staring at her as the doors roll closed.
INT. ROOM 2105 - CONTINUOUS
Dougherty and a uniformed cop keep an eye on Nick.
Anderson's on her way up.
Enter the Grim Reaper.
INT. 21ST FLOOR CORRIDOR - MOMENTS LATER
Dougherty waits outside the room as she heads up the hall.
Welcome back.
Kiss my ass, Bob.
He asked for you. By name.
She considers this.
What's the scene?
Suicide note. Name's Walker but we haven't made him yet. He's not talking except to say he's going off in... (checks watch) ...one minute.
She turns off the ringer on her cell and enters the room.
INT. ROOM 2105 - CONTINUOUS
Lydia stops, taking in the room: food, note, made beds, empty luggage rack.
Did he ask for help? No. Drunk?
LYDIA
Don't know. Attitude?
No dialogue.
She studies Nick from behind as he leans against the sealed window: posture, stance, attitude. EXT. LEDGE - CONTINUOUS Nick checks his watch.
8:41 AM. EXT. 45TH STREET - CONTINUOUS
A handful of REPORTERS and PHOTOGRAPHERS stand against the barricades photographing Nick and getting street interviews. ANGRY TRAFFIC GUY Anyone who creates this much
Elena Morales and her cameraman look up at Nick.
He jumps soon, we'll make evening news in London.
I give him an hour.
I'll take the under.
Twenty bucks?
She smiles. It's on.
I/E. ROOM 2105 / LEDGE - DAY
Two CSI TECHS dust for prints as Dougherty looks on. Lydia eyes the remains of Nick's breakfast, then glances at the techs working.
You process this yet? Yeah.
CSI TECH
She helps herself to some fries. Then she sets her cell phone by the TV, and puts in an earpiece attached to her radio. Taking a breath, she moves to the window and leans out.
Mr. Walker? I'm Detective Lydia Anderson. I understand you asked for me.
For the first time, Nick turns. threatening, attractive. She stands there: quiet, non-
Did you know part of Asia Minor was once called Lydia?
Do we know each other?
He shakes his head. frame. She settles comfortably onto the window
What brings us out here today?
Aside from the view?
She smiles. But then he steps forward, standing rigid, toes just off the lip of the ledge.
Trust me, this gazing into the abyss stuff isn't a good idea.
Today is the day everything changes. One way or another.
He pins her with an unwavering gaze, bringing every fiber of his soul to bear.
I am prepared to die. It's very important that you understand. She nods. Tell me.
I understand.
That's all for now.
He turns away, expecting her to go.
She doesn't.
Because you were with that jumper on the bridge last month and he died with the whole world watching.
This cuts right to her core: she's stunned, frozen into silence. Turning from the window, she finds Dougherty smiling at her.
Excellent work. Very impressive.
Clear the room.
Now hold on... My scene. Now!
My room.
I want space. Dougherty gives her a look as the techs file out.
This is on you.
INT. COMMAND CENTER - DAY

All the screens are now up showing a long-lensed view of Nick, plus wider views of the crowd, the street, and the Roosevelt, plus some TV feeds. Putting down a phone, a police techs turns to Marcus. We're getting a lot of static from the store owners.
The street stays locked up 'til I say so. I don't want this poor bastard falling on someone.
I/E. ROOM 2105 / LEDGE - DAY
Lydia takes a seat on the window again, watching Nick standing with his eyes closed, turned away, mumbling. Sensing something, he opens his eyes, sees her, and stops.
Are you praying?
My father died.
He holds up the picture from storage of him with Joey and their dad.
You have family? Parents.
Three brothers.
With three brothers, how do you end up a cop?
They're cops too. I was the rebel who got a degree and went into private practice.
Leather couch, matching chair, Miro lithograph?
She smiles.
(beat) It was dull as hell.
Now I'm here.
Any regrets?
Sure. Things happen. plans, God laughs. We make
Nick pauses, keeping his emotions in check.
You have a lot of friends on the force? Enough.
Not many I bet. Boy's Club. No women in the
You got something against cops?
EXT. WOODED HILLSIDE - ACCESS DOORWAY - DAY - CLOSE ON

Mike. Showered and sharply dressed, he's all energy as he paces while keeping an eye on the scene behind him where Nick escaped from the wastewater treatment plant. Officers put up police tape as detectives scour the area and a K-9 unit hunts around.
(into phone) Nothing at JFK? How about the border?
One of the detectives catches Mike's eye and shakes his head. Mike gives him a nod of acknowledgement.
(lowers his voice) Tom, I want you to reach out to everyone we know. Let's keep this internal. I want to find him first. Run everything you get through me.
INT. 21ST FLOOR CORRIDOR - DAY
Dougherty and the techs are standing in the hall when suddenly the room door opens. Dougherty straightens up as Lydia gets in his face.
Can you do your job or do you need to go?
Look, Lydia...
(pointing) I'm primary. You're secondary. You're here to support me. In or out: nothing in between.
Dougherty looks her over icily as the Techs watch stunned, waiting to see what's going to happen.
I'll do my job.
She turns and walks back into the room, leaving the door open behind her.
INT. ROOSEVELT HOTEL - ENGLANDER'S OFFICE - DAY

DAVID ENGLANDER, 50s, confident and serene, wearing a five thousand dollar suit, stands at a wall safe, strapping on a gold Brequet Classique 3137, then grabs a pair of massive emerald cuff-links while he talks on a headset.
I'll tell you what, Monica. I'll host a dinner for the gold circle donors. Use my name. Whatever you need. I mean, it's the children's
(beat) Hell, in this economy, it's the least I can do. Of course. Bye.
An ASSISTANT has appeared in the background with a welldressed 40-year-old Connecticut WASP.

Mr. Henry is here, sir. Englander closes the safe and walks through his massive open office in the 22nd floor of the Roosevelt, where architectural models of different developments are laid out on tables.
What do you want, Ted?
The man smiles nervously at Englander's approach.
I couldn't do it... (beat) I've dealt with the planning commission, but the councilman... it's too much of a risk for me.
Ted sets a Cartier box on Englander's desk.
Englander stares
The hell is that?
It's a gift... I'm sorry.
Englander opens the box, looks at the Pasha watch, and hurls it against the wall... WHAM!
I don't need a fucking watch, Ted! I need you to do the job you agreed to do! David...
The job that by not doing you are fucking me and my partners in a way that cannot be unfucked by some pathetic gesture in a red box which does absolutely nothing but make you feel better! I...
Tell me it's done. Please...
TELL ME IT'S FUCKING DONE!
